topic: A parent finds an old letter they wrote but never sent
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle, yellowed paper felt alien beneath Sarah’s fingertips. It was tucked inside a forgotten book, a relic from a time she barely remembered. As she unfolded the creases, the handwriting swam into focus—her own looping script, addressing a distant cousin she’d written to years ago, a torrent of sharp, unedited frustration spilling onto the page. A low, tight sound escaped her throat, a kind of strangled gasp. Her jaw clenched so hard she could feel the muscles vibrating beneath her skin. She crumpled the letter instantly, the paper protesting with a harsh rustle. "Why didn't I ever send this?" she muttered, the words tasting like ash. The silence of the room seemed to amplify the frantic thumping in her chest. She paced three steps, then stopped abruptly, staring at the crumpled mess as if it held the answer to a cosmic injustice.

[story 2]

Mark found the envelope tucked into the bottom of an old shoebox. The paper was thin, fragile, and undeniably his. Reading the contents, a wave of intense agitation washed over him. Every word was a carefully constructed indictment, a catalogue of grievances directed at someone he’d let slip away. He reread the passage about broken promises, his knuckles whitening as he gripped the edges of the envelope. A deep, hot flush crept up his neck. He shoved the letter back into the box with unnecessary force, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. He walked to the window, his breath coming in short, sharp bursts. The world outside seemed too bright, too indifferent to the raw, burning resentment churning inside him.

[story 3]

Eleanor discovered the correspondence during a spring cleaning. The letter, addressed to an old college friend, was a raw outpouring of unvoiced anger, detailing betrayals and profound disappointments. Her hands trembled as she held the document, the ink seeming to vibrate with the intensity of the emotions it contained. A deep, unsteady tremor ran through her frame. She let out a sharp, involuntary exhale, a sound bordering on a hiss. She dropped the letter onto the hardwood floor. It didn't shatter, but the sudden, violent act of discarding it felt necessary. Eleanor stood rigid, breathing deeply, trying to force the churning discomfort back down into a manageable, albeit still painful, knot in her stomach.
